The beige color indicates the pruning of the current step (layer) is conditioned on the pruning decisions made in the preceding steps (layers), which are marked in gray. ECoFLaP first performs the efficient coarse step to obtain the pruning ratio for each layer by leveraging the zeroth-order gradient, and then removes the uncritical weights in a layer-wise fashion in the fine step. find the sparse weight bWi at layer i via the corresponding local objective Li: bWi = arg maxS(Wi| bWi−1, D, Li), s.t., | bWi| |Wi| = pi, (1) where S(·) is the score function to compute the importance of the weight, bWi is denoted as the pruned weight from Wi, and pi% the desired sparsity at layer i. Basically, pi is the same for all layers in layer-wise pruning methods. In our proposed Efficient Coarse-to-Fine Layer-wise Pruning (ECoFLaP) framework, we estimate the optimal sparsity for each layer in the vision-language model by computing the global importance of model weights. One straightforward direction to obtain the layer sparsities is to perform global pruning on the model for the target global sparsity p by leveraging the global loss objective L, bW = arg maxS(W|D, L), s.t., | bW| |W| = p. (2) Then, we can estimate the layer sparsity via pi = | bWi|/|Wi|. However, this approach requires an expensive operation to obtain gradients for all weights in the large model and to extract the pruned weights via argmax, consuming enormous memory and computational resources. Therefore, we introduce an alternative numerical approach, which computes the keep ratio (1 - sparsity ratio) based on importance scores linearly, to avoid expensive operations that can estimate the global importance score efficiently. First, we obtain the importance score for each weight via the global objective function, namely, s = S(W|D, L), and then convert the scores to sparsity by three steps: (1) Find the total parameters that need to be selected based on p, (2) Normalize the scores, (3) Obtain the sparsity for each layer based on the number of parameters to be picked and the parameters of this layer. The example to determine the sparsity ratio for the ith layer of the model is shown below, normalize(si, s) = siPs, (3) pi = 1− (normalize(si, s) · Nselect) |Wi| , where Nselect = (1− p) · |W|. (4) We then inject the derived sparsity ratios back to Equation 1 to prune the model layer-wisely. We also introduce a hyperparameter pmax to control the maximum sparsity for each layer to avoid the layer collapse, which is discovered by previous literature (Tanaka et al., 2020) that the global pruning approach may remove all the parameters for one layer. To incorporate this hyperparameter in the Equation 3, we simply pre-pick the parameters for each layer to satisfy the max sparsity condition, subtracting the Nselect with the number of pre-picked parameters, and start the algorithm. In our experiments, we find using the same sparsity ratio for the layers in one block (such as the feed-forward layer and attention layer in a transformer block) makes the results more robust, so we also extend the 5Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 algorithm to determine group sparsity ratios by via group scores (aggregating all the layer scores in a block). For the layer-wise pruning step, we utilize Wanda, which combines the input activation of the layer with the weight magnitude as the local importance score, to remove uncritical parameters based on the obtained layer sparsity ratios. 4.2 Z EROTH -ORDER GLOBAL INFORMATION (a) Zeroth-order Gradient(b) Forward-forward Algorithm Figure 3: Illustration of (a) the zeroth-order gradi- ent in weight space, and (b) the forward-forward algorithm. In the previous section, we introduce our coarse- to-fine pruning framework to determine the prun- ing ratios for layer-wise pruning. Note that our goal is to prune models with billions of param- eters, so we again focus on the one-shot setting in this step without any iterative refinement. To this end, our first attempt is to use the first-order backward gradient with weight magnitude (Le- Cun et al., 1989), |W| · |▽WL(W, D)|, as the global importance score, and the performance improvement demonstrates the effectiveness of our framework (Please see Table 1 and Figure 4). However, even though the first-order backward gradient is still tractable for most models. like less than 5B parameters, 7B LLaMA (Touvron et al., 2023) requires more than 40GB even with float16 weight type and batch size being one. To further reduce the cost of computing gradients, we instead compute the zeroth-order approximated gradient by replacing the backpropagation with the forward-forward algorithm (Hinton, 2022; Malladi et al., 2023). We take the ith layer of a model as an example, the zeroth-order gradient of Wi can be computed by perturbing the weight with Gaussian noises twice, that is, ∥▽Wi L(Wi, D)∥2 = Ed∼D[Ez∼N(0,1)[|L(Wi + ϵz, d) − L(Wi − ϵz, d) 2ϵ |]] (5) We hence use ∥▽Wi L(Wi, D)∥2 as the importance score of Wi. Note that we do not multiply it with the weight magnitude as solely using the approximated gradients works better empirically. The computation of Equation 5 is extremely memory-efficient and the exact process for the ith layer is shown in the following: (1) First, we choose a random seed and then sample a noise z (now we use 2x of memory of “this layer”), (2) we modify the model’s weight with W + ϵz and compute the loss change L(W + ϵz, d), (3) we then sample the same noise z as the first step (by setting the same random seed), and we modify the model’s weight with W − ϵz, which can be done by subtracting 2ϵz from the previous weight, and compute L(W − ϵz, d), (4) obtain the gradient norm by (L(W + ϵz) − L(W − ϵz))/(2ϵ) (all terms are scalars). From the above process, for an N-layer network, we only need (N+1) times of one-layer memory, which is significantly memory-efficient than storing the whole gradients that cause 2N times of one-layer memory. This explains why our approach uses much less memory than first-order based methods, which also need to cache input activations. One may suspect we need to sample many noises to capture the loss of the landscape and to estimate the gradient accurately. However, our experiments and the previous studies (Malladi et al., 2023) show that one noise is sufficient. We describe our approach in detail in Algorithm 1. 5 E XPERIMENTAL SETUP We report evaluation metrics and more experimental details in Appendix C. Architectures. We use multiple uni- and multi-modal architectures for experiments: the encoder- decoder vision of BLIP-2 (Li et al., 2023c), composed of pre-trained EV A-ViT (ViT-g/14 from EV A-CLIP) (Sun et al., 2023b) and FlanT5 (Chung et al., 2022), is used for most experiments and ablation studies. And, we also extend our approach to BLIP (Li et al., 2022) with ViT (Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) and BERT (Devlin et al., 2019) backbones. In addition, we evaluate our approach solely on unimodal vision and NLP tasks with EV A-ViT, FlanT5, and LLaMA backbones. Evaluation Datasets. We evaluate the zero-shot ability of BLIP-2 on various datasets after pruning, such as VQAv2, OK-VQA, and GQA for visual question answering, NoCaps for image captioning, and Flickr30k for image-text retrieval. For unimodal models, we evaluate FlanT5 on MMLU, evaluate LLaMA 7B with WikiText, and evaluate EV A-ViT with ImageNet-1k. Baselines. We compare ECoFLaP to several global pruning and layer-wise pruning approaches. For Global Magnitude Pruning, we perform global pruning based on the magnitude of the weight. Gradient-based Pruning is an iterative global pruning approach, where we use the multiplication of the first-order gradient and the weight magnitude as the importance score and prune the model to target sparsity in 3 iterations. SparseGPT is a layer-wise Hessian-based method. Wanda is also a layer-wise approach utilizing the multiplication of the weight magnitude and the norm of input activation as the local importance score. The comparison of the above pruning methods and their importance metric can be found in Table 8. We also compare our method with UPop, which prunes and re-trains the vision-language model simultaneously in a unified progressive pruning manner. 6 R ESULTS 6.1 M AIN EXPERIMENTAL RESULTS We demonstrate the zero-shot performance on various datasets using BLIP-2 pruned by our ECoFLaP and baselines at a 0.5 sparsity ratio in Table 1. And we compare our approach with UPop using BLIP backbone on NLVR2 and COCO captions under the fine-tuning and non-fine-tuning settings in Table 3. We also provide the comparison of our approach and Wanda at various sparsities on both multi- and uni-modal models in Figure 4. We summarize our observations as follows: ECoFLaP consistently outperforms Global Magnitude Pruning (GMP) and Gradient-based Pruning as shown in Table 1. GMP fails to generate meaningful results at 0.5 sparsity. In particular, we find that GMP suffers from pruning the vision module, EV A-ViT, so the degraded visual repre- sentation harms the multimodal model performance. Gradient-based Pruning performs significantly better by selecting crucial weights based on gradient information of the whole model, but it requires a larger memory than GMP due to the full pass to compute the gradients. ECoFLaP outperforms SoTA layer-wise pruning baselinessince our ECoFLaP is able to exploit the global information. Our approach with zeroth-order gradient consistently achieves better performance against Wanda on all tasks and metrics by 1.1%, 2.3%, and 1.0%, on visual question answering, image captioning, and image retrieval tasks, respectively, showing the effectiveness of our coarse- to-fine framework. Furthermore, we highlight that the gap between Wanda and ECoFLaP becomes larger when the sparsity increases to 0.6, where ECoFLaP outperforms Wanda by 9.6% on VQA (shown in Figure 4a). Our approach also surpasses SparseGPT by 1.4% of relative improvement on the average score. Note that our zeroth-order ECoFLaP uses on-par memory budgets compared to layer-wise pruning methods, Wanda and SparseGPT, by avoiding expensive backpropagation computation. Additional results on the combination of ECoFLaP and SparseGPT are in Table 9. ECoFLaP surpasses baselines on pruning CLIP models by a significant margin. Beyond pruning modularized VL models, ECoFLaP can also work well with joint VL models like CLIP. In Table 2, we evaluate Wanda, SparseGPT, and our approach on CLIP by measuring the zero-shot accuracy on 11 classification tasks before and after pruning over the sparsity 0.4, and our ECoFLaP excels against baselines while effectively mitigating the degeneration of zero-shot accuracy through the model pruning. In addition, we assess the impact of using global scores for computing layer sparsity ratios by comparing it with the approach using local scores derived from layer-wise pruning methods (namely, Wanda and SparseGPT). The result shows that the strategy of using local scores leads to a decrease in performance compared to leveraging a uniform sparsity ratio. This observation emphasizes the ineffectiveness of employing local scores for assessing layer importance (Please see our motivation in Figure 1). Table 3: Performance comparison on BLIP at 0.5 sparsity on NLVR2 and COCO cap- tions. Method NLVR2 COCO cap.val test CIDEr SPICE Full Model 82.3 83.6 133.3 23.8 w/o fine-tuningUPop 76.9 77.8 - -Wanda 78.3 78.1 97.1 18.4ECoFLaP79.1 79.2 111.0 20.3 w/ fine-tuningUPop 80.3 81.1 128.9 23.3ECoFLaP81.8 82.5 132.3 23.8 ECoFLaP outperforms the multimodal model prun- ing baseline, UPop, in both non-fine-tuning and fine- tuning cases as shown in Table 3. Note that UPop requires re-training the backbone model in the pruning phase, which is significantly expensive in memory and computation, especially for large models. On the con- trary, ECoFLaP finds an important subset of weights in the model in a single shot and then fine-tunes the pruned model. We also emphasize that our approach general- izes to different architectures and datasets, where BLIP uses a BERT-encoder for NLVR2 (classification) and a BERT-decoder for COCO (image captioning). ECoFLaP also generalizes well to unimodal tasks/architectures. In Figure 4b and Figure 4c, we visualize the accuracy of MMLU with FlanT5 and ImageNet-1k with EV A-ViT after pruning with various sparsity ratios (from 0.1 to 0.6). Since Wanda applies the same pruning ratio to all layers, performance degrades with increasing sparsity ( ∼ 40% ↑), which is catastrophic for some layers containing much more informative weights than others. Alternatively, the performance degradation caused by pruning weights can be significantly mitigated by proposing adaptive layer-wise pruning with dynamic sparsity for each layer based on estimated global weight importance. Furthermore, as shown in Table 7, our approach shows the ability to generalize to larger LLM, where we attain 10.6% relative improvement over Wanda when evaluated on WikiText using perplexity with LLaMA 7B. 8Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 Table 4: The ablation on the maximum sparsity. BLIP-2 (p= 0.5) Number of Samples 16 32 64 128 VQA 58.2 58.4 58.3 58.4 Table 6: The ablation on number of noises (total samples = 32). BLIP-2 (p= 0.5) Number of noises 1 4 8 32 VQA 58.4 58.1 58.0 58.1 6.2 A BLATIONS AND VISUALIZATION We provide ablation studies of our zeroth-order ECoFLaP on maximum sparsity for each layer pM , the number of data |D|, and the number of perturbed noises per sample. We report the zero-shot VQA accuracy obtained from the BLIP-T5 with 0.5 total sparsity. Note that we use the numerical method to obtain the sparsity ratios for experiments (Please see Equation 3). Maximum Sparsity. First, we observe that the maximum sparsity is critical as it affects the results considerably, and the optimal value happens at0.6, which is a conservative value given the target ratio is 0.5. Similar to the findings discovered by Tanaka et al. (2020), the algorithm might suffer layer collapse when the maximum sparsity is not set (pM = 1). We find it simple but effective to introduce this hyperparameter as we would like to avoid using the iterative solution proposed by Tanaka et al. (2020) because we are targeting the one-shot pruning. Based on the result shown in Table 4, we simply set pM = p + 0.1 throughout our experiments. Number of Data and Noises. In the next ablation experiment, we focus on understanding how many samples and noises we need to estimate the gradient with zeroth-order optimization. We first ablate the effect of the number of data by fixing the number of noises per sample to be one, and the result in Table 5 shows that our approach is robust to the number of samples across 16 to 128, and we thus using 32 samples for better efficiency. Next, we explore if having a more accurate gradient estimation by sampling more noises can improve the accuracy. In this ablation, we make sure to forward through the model the same number of times when using different numbers of noises, meaning that we cut the number of data to 1/n when we increase the number of noises to n. The result in Table 6 demonstrates that using more data samples is more effective compared to sampling more noises when the budget is fixed. Visualization and Analysis. We also provide the visualization of the sparsity ratios obtained by ECoFLaP (Figure 6) and the loss landscape of BLIP-2 (Figure 5) to justify the use of zeroth-order optimization. In Figure 6, we observe that our ECoFLaP variants allocate sparsity ratios in a similar distribution, in favor of lower sparsity ratios for the visual model and higher sparsity ratios for the language model, and this alignment provides evidence that the zeroth-order gradient obtained by the forward-forward algorithm can be the accurate but cheaper alternative to the first-order gradient in importance estimation. The landscape shown in Figure 5 presents that BLIP-2 is located at a smooth basin with a single local minimum. This loss landscape justifies the success of utilizing zeroth-order optimization as we avoid sampling weights located in other basins that may cause an inaccurate gradient estimation. We also present a qualitative analysis of the pruned model in Appendix E. 7 C ONCLUSION This paper has investigated the challenges of pruning large-scale vision-language models due to the dilemma between immense memory/computational overhead from global pruning and the suboptimal model performance of layer-wise pruning. Existing global pruning methods require an expensive backpropagation process to compute the inverse Hessian to obtain global weight importance, which makes these approaches infeasible for current large LLMs and large LVLMs. On the other hand, layer-wise pruning approaches are highly efficient by pruning weights based on layer-wise local information, but often suffer from a lack of global importance, resulting in a significant performance degeneration. To address the limitations of these pruning approaches while enjoying their merits, we propose ECoFLaP that adaptively finds the optimal sparsity for each layer by efficiently estimating the global weight importance, and then accurately prunes the vision-language model in a layer-wise manner dependent on the obtained pruning ratios. We utilize the zeroth-order optimization to obtain the gradients with forward pass only and achieve superior performance over multiple uni- and multi- modal tasks against recent layer-wise pruning methods by a significant margin, while consuming only around 40% of GPU memory compared to the iterative global pruning approach. We hope our new efficient pruning can help deploy quality yet compact vision-language models. 9Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 8 R EPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT Our codes are based on the publicly available LA VIS (Li et al., 2023b), Wanda (Sun et al., 2023a), MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021), UPop (Shi et al., 2023), and CoOp (Zhou et al., 2021). The experimental setup and details can be found in Section 5 and Appendix C, respectively. We also have made our code publicly available. In Proceedings of the IEEE International Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR), 2022b. 3 Deyao Zhu, Jun Chen, Xiaoqian Shen, Xiang Li, and Mohamed Elhoseiny. Minigpt-4: En- hancing vision-language understanding with advanced large language models. arXiv preprint arXiv:2304.10592, 2023. 1 16Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 Algorithm 1: Efficient Coarse-to-Fine Layer-wise Pruning Input: weights of the vision model Wv, weights of the language model Wl, calibration dataset D, target sparsity p, maximum sparsity per layer pmax Output: The pruned weights bW v and bW l // Coarse step: determine the sparsity ratios via global information 1 s ← S(Wv, Wl, D, L) ; // get the global importance score 2 p ← getSparityFromScores(s, Wv, Wl, p, pmax) ; // obtain the sparsity via Equation 3 // Fine step: perform layer-wise pruning 3 bW v , bW l = {}, {} // We initialize the 0th weight to be the identity matrix, depicting the fact that the input of the first layer is the raw input. 4 Wv 0 ← I 5 for i = 1 to M do 6 bW v i = arg maxS(Wv i | bW v i−1, D, Lv i , pv i ) ; // prune vision weights layer by layer 7 bW v = bW v + { bW v i } 8 Wl 0 ← Wv M 9 for i = 1 to L do 10 bW l i = arg maxS(Wl i| bW l i−1, D, Ll i, pl i) ; // prune language weights layer by layer 11 bW l = bW v + { bW l i} 12 return bW v , bW l Figure 5: The loss landscape of the BLIP-2 model. 0 100 200 300 400 500 600 Layer index 0.25 0.30 0.35 0.40 0.45 0.50 0.55 0.60Sparsity Separation between language and vision model first-order grad zeroth-order grad Figure 6: Sparsity ratios obtained by ECoFLaP. A T HE LOSS LANDSCAPE We present a visualization of the loss landscape (Li et al., 2018) for BLIP-2 in Figure 5, where we achieve this by perturbing the pre-trained BLIP-2 model 2500 times to approximate the parameter space and using 256 samples from CC3M to compute the loss. Our visualization reveals a distinctive corn-shaped loss landscape, which is characterized by a single basin centered around the pre-trained weight of the BLIP-2. Moreover, the landscape is surprisingly smooth with a linear slope around the pre-trained weight. This smooth and corn-shaped loss landscape explains why we can estimate the gradient with zeroth-order optimization this successfully, as we avoid sampling weights situated in other basins that could lead to inaccurate gradient estimation. B R ESULTS ON LLAMA We further assess our approach’s performance using perplexity on the WikiText dataset with LLaMA, a prominent open-source LLM. The results on the 7B model presented in Table 7 demonstrate that ECoFLaP achieves a noteworthy 10.6% relative improvement compared to Wanda when operating 17Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 Table 7: Evaluation of Wanda and ECoFLaP on LLaMA 7B at 0.6 sparsity by perplexity on WikiText. LLaMA @ 0.6 sparsity Perplexity Full Model 7.26 Wanda 10.68 ECoFLaP + first-order gradient 10.16 + zeroth-order gradient 9.83 at 0.6 sparsity. Notably, in this experiment, we find that employing a larger ϵ value in Equation 5 substantially enhances performance. This observation suggests that the zeroth-order estimation can benefit from exploring regions further from the target weight, a capability not inherent in gradients obtained through backpropagation. C E XPERIMENTAL DETAILS Evaluation Datasets and Metrics. We evaluate the zero-shot ability of BLIP-2 on various datasets after pruning. We use VQAv2 (Goyal et al., 2016), OK-VQA (Marino et al., 2019), and GQA (Hudson & Manning, 2019) for visual question answering, NoCaps (Agrawal et al., 2019) for image captioning, and Flickr30k (Plummer et al., 2015) for image-text retrieval. For BLIP, we evaluate the performance change of the BLIP, fine-tuned on NLVR2 (Suhr et al., 2019) and COCO captions (Chen et al., 2015). For unimodal models, we evaluate FlanT5 on 57 tasks in MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021), evaluate LLaMA 7B with WikiText (Merity et al., 2016), and evaluate EV A-ViT on image classification task with ImageNet-1k (Deng et al., 2009). We report accuracy for question-answering datasets, NLVR2, ImageNet-1k, and tasks in MMLU. Next, we use CIDEr and SPICE to evaluate image captioning tasks and use TR@1 (top-1 text recall) and IR@1 (top-1 image recall) for image retrieval tasks. Lastly, we report the model’s perplexity of WikiText in evaluating the LLaMA. Calibration Datasets. Our approach utilizes a small subset (128 samples) of a dataset as the calibration data from CC3M (Sharma et al., 2018), ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) and C4 (Raffel et al., 2019) for calibrating BLIP-2, unimodal EV A-ViT, and unimodal FlanT5 (and LLaMA). We use NLVR2 and COCO captions as the calibration data for fine-tuning the BLIP backbone. Dataset splits. For VQAv2, OK-VQA, and GQA, we use val, test, and test-dev split, respectively. We use the validation set for NoCaps and the test set for Flickr30k. In our BLIP experiments, we report results on both val and test set for NLVR2, while we use the Karpathy test split (Karpathy & Fei-Fei, 2014) for COCO captions. In our unimodal experiments, we use the publicly available test set for MMLU, and we use the validation set for ImageNet-1k. For WikiText, we report the perplexity on the validation set. Datasets and Metrics for CLIP experiments. We use a diverse set of 11 image classification datasets to evaluate the model’s ability to recognize generic and specific objects: Caltech101 (Fei-Fei et al., 2004), DTD (Cimpoi et al., 2013), EuroSAT (Helber et al., 2017), FGVCAircraft (Maji et al., 2013), Food101 (Bossard et al., 2014), ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009), OxfordFlowers (Nilsback & Zisserman, 2008), OxfordPets (Parkhi et al., 2012), StanfordCars (Krause et al., 2013), SUN397 (Xiao et al., 2010), UCF101 (Soomro et al., 2012). We report accuracy for each dataset in Table 2. Seed. The default seed option for each codebase is used for evaluation: seed 42 for all the experiments for BLIP-2, FlanT5, and EV A-ViT, seed 0 for the LLaMA experiment, and seed 1 for the CLIP experiment. Hyperparameters. For the coarse step, we set pM = p + 0.1, |D| = 32, and the number of noises to be 1 for ECoFLaP with zeroth-order gradient. ϵ is set to 1e−3 except for LLaMA, where we find 1e−1 works better, but we almost did not tune this hyperparameter. For ECoFLaP with first-order gradient, we use |D| = 128. Resources. All the experiments are done with one 40GB A100 or one 48GB A6000, except for ECoFLaP with first-order gradient on LLaMA we use 2x 48GB A6000. 18Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 Table 8: The comparison of different methods in terms of their category and used importance measure. Methods Global or Layer-wise Importance Measure Global Magnitude Pruning Global ∥W∥ij Gradient-based Pruning Global ∥W∥ij · ∥▽Wi L∥ij SparseGPT Layer-wise [∥W∥2 /diag (XX T + λI)−1 ]ij Wanda Layer-wise ∥Wij ∥ · ∥Xj ∥2 ECoFLaP w/ Wanda and zeroth-order Mixed First Stage: ∥▽Wi L∥ij Second Stage: ∥Wij ∥ · ∥Xj ∥2 Table 9: Comparison of SparseGPT and ECoFLaP (with SparseGPT) on the zero-shot performance with BLIP-2 at 0.6 sparsity. We report accuracy for visual question answering, CIDEr and SPICE for image captioning, and TR@1 (text recall) and IR@1 (image recall) for image retrieval. We also compute the macro average score over tasks and the GPU memory usage for each method. Method Sparsity Method’s Mem. Usage (GB) Visual Question Answering Image Captioning Image retrievalMacro Avg.VQAv2 OK-VQA GQA NoCaps Flickr30k Accuracy CIDEr SPICE TR@1 IR@1 SparseGPT 60% 9.04 48.7 27.9 35.1 101.0 13.4 95.2 85.0 51.8 ECoFLaP w/ SparseGPT 60% 9.05 50.4 27.9 36.3 105.3 14.0 95.3 86.1 53.1 D EC OFLAP ON SPARSE GPT As the key contribution of our method for efficient estimation of layer-adaptive pruning rate for layer-wise pruning is orthogonal to SparseGPT, ECoFLaP can also be combined with SparseGPT. As shown in Table 9, EcoFLaP (w/ SparseGPT) consistently achieves superior performance over multiple VL tasks. Additionally, the performance improvement seems relatively smaller compared to ECoFLaP with Wanda (i.e., original EcoFLaP). We hypothesize that this is because the reweighting mechanism in SparseGPT is somewhat correlated to our dynamic sparsity ratios, where both of them are important to preserve the performance in high sparsity ratios. E Q UALITATIVE COMPARISON OF THE MODEL BEFORE AND AFTER PRUNING We conducted an analysis comparing the predictions of the Full model (before pruning) and the model pruned by ECoFlaP (with 0.5 sparsity) on the GQA datasets, and presented three illustrative examples in Table 10. We found one interesting observation: out of 12578 questions, the Full model correctly answered 1219 questions that our model did not, yet our model also accurately responded to 1072 questions where the Full model failed to respond (there are 4322 questions that both models answer correctly). This indicates that pruning does not always lead to performance degradation; in some instances, it may even enhance model robustness for certain questions. During our review of incorrect predictions by either our model or the Full model, we noticed that most were conceptually similar to the ground truth. This suggests that the pruned model still retains most of the capability. Furthermore, in some cases, such as with the ’horses running’ example, both models provided answers that might be considered correct by many humans, even though they differ from the ground truth. 19Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024 Table 10: Qualitative examples to compare the predictions generated by the Full Model and the ECoFLaP-pruned model. We pick three example image-question pairs from GQA datasets. We observe the model after pruning does not always perform worse than the Full Model. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Introduces ECoFLaP, a two-stage coarse-to-fine, one-shot, unstructured pruning framework for large vision-language models (LVLMs) that allocates adaptive per-layer sparsity using globally-informed scores computed efficiently via a zeroth-order (forward-only) gradient approximation, then performs layer-wise pruning using local importance. Demonstrates consistent gains over state-of-the-art pruning methods (Wanda, SparseGPT) and a multimodal pruning baseline (UPop) across VL and unimodal tasks, especially at high sparsity, while reducing GPU memory during pruning (up to ~40% vs first-order gradient methods). Shows broad applicability to BLIP/BLIP-2, CLIP, FlanT5, LLaMA, and EVA-ViT with multiple benchmarks; releases code and analyses (loss landscape, sparsity patterns, ablations).
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Target: Efficient, one-shot, adaptive layer-wise unstructured pruning for LVLMs (focus on BLIP/BLIP-2 style: ViT visual encoder + LM, with Q-Former kept intact as it is small ~5%).
- Coarse step (global importance and sparsity allocation):
  - Compute global importance per layer/group without Hessian or backprop using a zeroth-order gradient norm: for layer weights W_i, estimate ||∇_{W_i} L|| via symmetric perturbations with Gaussian noise z and two forward passes: (L(W_i+εz)−L(W_i−εz))/(2ε); aggregate over small calibration data. Typically 1 noise/sample suffices; default |D|=32; ε≈1e−3 (1e−1 for LLaMA).
  - Convert importance scores to layer/group keep ratios: normalize scores, allocate N_select=(1−p)*|W| kept parameters proportionally, derive per-layer sparsity p_i=1−(normalized_i*N_select)/|W_i|. Enforce a per-layer max sparsity p_max (set to p+0.1) to avoid layer collapse; optionally share sparsity within transformer blocks (e.g., attention/FFN).
  - A first-order variant using |W|*|∇W L| is possible but uses much more memory.
- Fine step (layer-wise pruning):
  - Apply layer-wise unstructured pruning using Wanda’s local importance (|W_ij|*||X_j||_2) per layer to match the computed p_i. Also compatible with SparseGPT as an alternative fine step.
- Practical details: single-shot pruning; Q-Former unpruned; forward-forward scheme greatly reduces memory to ~ (N+1) one-layer memories; uses small calibration subsets; hyperparameters lightly tuned; ablations on p_max, |D|, and number of noises.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Architectures: BLIP-2 (EVA-CLIP ViT-g/14 visual encoder + FlanT5), BLIP (ViT + BERT encoder/decoder), CLIP (joint VL), and unimodal EVA-ViT, FlanT5, LLaMA 7B.
- Datasets and tasks:
  - Vision-language zero-shot (BLIP-2): VQAv2 (val acc), OK-VQA (test acc), GQA (test-dev acc); NoCaps (val CIDEr/SPICE); Flickr30k (TR@1, IR@1).
  - BLIP fine-tuning: NLVR2 (val/test acc), COCO captions (Karpathy test CIDEr/SPICE).
  - Unimodal: ImageNet-1k (EVA-ViT top-1 val acc), MMLU (FlanT5, avg acc over 57 tasks), WikiText (LLaMA 7B perplexity).
  - CLIP zero-shot: 11 classification datasets (Caltech101, DTD, EuroSAT, FGVCAircraft, Food101, ImageNet, OxfordFlowers, OxfordPets, StanfordCars, SUN397, UCF101; accuracy).
- Calibration data: small subsets; CC3M for BLIP-2, ImageNet for EVA-ViT, C4 for FlanT5/LLaMA. Default |D|=32 for zeroth-order, 128 for first-order.
- Baselines: Global Magnitude Pruning, iterative Gradient-based Global Pruning (|W|*|∇W L|), SparseGPT (layer-wise Hessian-based), Wanda (layer-wise magnitude*activation), UPop (progressive pruning with retraining).
- Key results:
  - BLIP-2 @ 0.5 sparsity: ECoFLaP (zeroth-order) macro avg 58.0 vs Wanda 57.2 and SparseGPT 57.4; similar/practical memory (~8.9GB) vs first-order (~22.4GB). Larger gains at 0.6 sparsity (e.g., +9.6% VQA over Wanda).
  - BLIP vs UPop @ 0.5: Without FT, NLVR2 val 79.1 vs 76.9; with FT, NLVR2 81.8/82.5; COCO CIDEr 132.3 (near full 133.3).
  - CLIP @ 0.4: ECoFLaP+SparseGPT avg 61.0 vs SparseGPT 58.7; ECoFLaP+Wanda 56.0 vs Wanda 47.2.
  - Unimodal: Improved robustness on MMLU and ImageNet vs Wanda across sparsities; LLaMA 7B @0.6 sparsity: perplexity 9.83 vs Wanda 10.68 (~10.6% relative improvement).
- Resources: single 40GB A100 or 48GB A6000; LLaMA first-order needed 2×48GB A6000. Ablations on p_max, number of calibration samples, and noises; visualizations of loss landscape and learned sparsity distributions.
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Unstructured pruning; real end-to-end speedups depend on sparse kernel support and are not reported.
- Requires small but task-relevant calibration data; performance may depend on data choice and ε; hyperparameters (p_max, ε) influence stability and need light tuning.
- One-shot pruning may be suboptimal vs iterative pruning+retraining in some settings; fine step still needs access to activations (for Wanda) and may add memory cost.
- Q-Former is left unpruned; structured component/latency-aware pruning is not explored.
- Evaluation focuses on selected backbones and datasets; larger LVLMs and broader modalities (audio/video) are not studied; energy/latency/carbon benefits are not empirically quantified.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Integrate hardware-aware and structured sparsity (blocks, heads, channels) for real speedups; benchmark latency/energy on sparse runtimes.
- Jointly combine with quantization, low-rank adaptation, token/layer pruning for compound compression.
- Adaptive calibration (data selection, ε and p_max auto-tuning), and theoretical analysis of zeroth-order estimation under different loss landscapes.
- Extend to larger and more diverse multimodal models (video, audio-language) and instruction-tuned LVLMs.
- Iterative or light retraining variants that refine globally-informed sparsity while keeping efficiency.
- Explore alternative global signals (Fisher/curvature approximations, learned allocators) and better grouping strategies across modules; dynamic sparsity schedules during fine-tuning.
- Prune additional modules (e.g., Q-Former) and study interaction with cross-modal alignment losses; integrate with knowledge distillation for accuracy recovery.

new_method.method:
Motivation
- Problem: Multimodal VLMs allocate identical inference compute across inputs, though inputs differ widely in which tokens, layers, precisions, and KV-cache timesteps matter. Existing techniques optimize only one axis (depth-only early exit, static pruning/quantization) or train for efficiency but do not plan runtime execution per input.
- Opportunity: The Modality Focusing Hypothesis (MFH) suggests a small set of decisive, modality-general cues dominate accuracy. Fast, per-input estimates of (a) how shared a token’s evidence is across modalities and (b) how predictable activations are across layers can guide token routing, dynamic halting, precision, and KV policies. 
- Gap: No budget-aware, single-pass controller exists that jointly chooses token routing, depth, mixed precision, and KV-cache schedules per input and per decoding step, trained stably without RL and aligned to real-device latency. Prior work (MuE, MULTIFLOW/ECoFLaP, Q-VLM, FLM, SPARC/MFH) individually addresses depth, static sparsity, static PTQ, training-time efficiency, or alignment—but not joint, per-input, device-aware inference scheduling.

Methodology
Overview
- Q-SHIFT: a single warm pass estimates per-token sharedness and conditional activation entropy. A budget-conditioned controller then selects discrete actions across four levers—token routing, depth/halting, mixed precision, and KV-cache policy—using QVI (Quantized Variational Inference) for stable, variance-free training. A device latency oracle ensures plans meet target budgets; a risk head enables safe backoff.

1) Fast sharedness and predictability signals (warm pass)
- Sharedness per token/patch s(i):
  - Cross-attention agreement: compute bidirectional image↔text attention maps in 1–2 shallow layers; score symmetric similarity (e.g., Jensen–Shannon divergence, head-averaged); higher agreement ⇒ more modality-general.
  - Sparse token–patch grouping: SPARC-style non-softmax grouping with min–max normalization and fixed threshold σ=1/P to produce language-grouped vision embeddings; token–group cosine amplifies cross-modal support.
  - Cross-modal co-saliency: cheap, head-averaged attention concentration over overlapping top-k regions (no GradCAM), serving as a tie-breaker when s(i) is ambiguous.
- Predictability via conditional activation entropy:
  - Fake-quantize activations in two consecutive shallow layers (per modality) with small codebooks; compute conditional entropy H(A_{l+1}|A_l) over quant bins. Low conditional entropy ⇒ stable propagation ⇒ safer low-bit, potential early exit.
- Aggregation: a tiny fusion MLP ingests per-token s_text/s_vis, layer-wise entropy histograms/percentiles, sequence lengths, and builds compact features for the controller.

2) Budget-conditioned, single-shot planner (core novelty)
- Inputs: target budget B (latency/FLOPs/energy), seq lengths, histograms/percentiles of sharedness and entropy; device latency oracle.
- Discrete action codebooks (hardware-aligned):
  - Token routing: keep ratios per modality {25, 50, 75, 100%}; ToMe-like merge granularity {2, 4, 8} for low-s tokens; cross-attn restricted to retained tokens.
  - Depth/halting: per-modality early exits at {1/4, 1/2, 3/4, full}; decoder schedules for when to evaluate logits and when to raise precision.
  - Mixed precision: per-block/head W/A bitwidths {W8A8, W6A6, W4A4}; attention-head drop allowed if head mass concentrates on high-s tokens.
  - KV-cache schedule: KV precision {8, 6, 4} bits; head-drop and step-shared scales when conditional entropy is low; eviction of low-attention tokens in long generations.
- Latency oracle: TVM/TensorRT per-kernel microbenchmarks and sequence-length/bitwidth-aware packing costs map action tuples ↔ predicted latency; used as differentiable constraints via a surrogate loss and as a hard check at runtime.
- Safety backoff: a risk head monitors logit-margin deltas and entropy drift; on trip, progressively unmask tokens, raise precision, or defer exits—without re-planning.

3) Stable discrete policy learning via QVI (novel training)
- Policy parameterization: each lever has a low-D latent z producing logits over its discrete codebook; the joint plan is a Cartesian product of a handful of action tuples.
- QVI cubature: replace RL/Gumbel with QVI’s optimal-quantization grids to compute a weighted objective over a small set of action tuples, yielding variance-free gradients and controllable bias. Begin with bias-reduced RQVI, then switch to QVI for stability.
- Objective: L = L_task + λb·(Latency−B)_+ + λm·MemReg + λe·ExitReg + λkv·KVReg + L_align.
  - L_task: VQA CE, NLVR2 CE, retrieval ITC/ITM, captioning NLL; plus MuE-style per-layer decoder CE to keep intermediate states predictive.
  - L_align: protect high-s tokens via contrastive losses (preserves retrieval/grounding under aggressive pruning/quantization).
  - Regularizers: encourage monotone precision schedules and bounded head drop; penalize frequent backoffs.
- Device curriculum: randomize B and latency profiles per batch to learn device-conditioned, budget-aware plans.

4) Runtime components and kernels
- SharednessHead: attaches after first 1–2 transformer layers (vision/text) to compute s and entropy; exports top-K indices and ToMe merges.
- HaltingGates: per-token gates in fusion/self-attn to implement planned exits per modality and decoder step.
- PrecisionAdapters: wrap attention/linear ops with pre-instantiated W8/W6/W4 and activation quantizers; dispatch tokens by chosen bitwidth to fused kernels; no JIT compilation at inference.
- KVAdapters: dynamic KV quantizers with optional head-drop and step-shared scales (low-entropy regime) to reduce overhead.
- Packing + oracle: contiguous packing by modality and bitwidth; runtime validates plan against oracle, triggers backoff if exceeded.

5) Integration and training recipe
- Base models: LLaVA/BLIP-family; freeze backbone; train controller + SharednessHead + adapters (<1% params); optional few-shot activation-range calibration (à la Q-VLM) to stabilize low-bit paths.
- Data: use standard VLM mixtures (e.g., LLaVA) plus task subsets (VQA v2, NLVR2, Flickr/COCO retrieval, COCO cap).
- Evaluation: accuracy/CE/ITC/ITM vs measured latency/energy; KV footprint; Expected Time Reduction Rate; robustness on clutter/OOD; long-input decoding stability. Ablate levers, QVI vs RL/Gumbel, with/without MFH cues and L_align.

How this differs from prior work
- Joint, per-input multi-axis scheduling: simultaneously routes tokens, halts depth, selects mixed precision, and plans KV policies under a single budgeted controller; prior work optimizes one axis (MuE depth-only, MULTIFLOW/ECoFLaP static sparsity, Q-VLM static PTQ).
- Sharedness-/entropy-aware execution: turns MFH/SPARC signals into real-time scheduling signals; prior works use them for training/teachers, not inference planning.
- QVI for discrete inference planning: stable, variance-free optimization over discrete codebooks; not used previously for VLM inference policies.
- Decoder-time adaptivity: KV precision/head-drop tied to attention on high-s tokens and conditional entropy across steps; missing in pruning/quantization/exiting baselines.
- Hardware-aligned planning: device latency oracle, fused integer kernels, and token-by-bitwidth packing convert FLOP cuts into wall-clock savings across GPUs/NPUs.

Expected impact
- Efficiency: 35–60% latency reduction with ≤1–2% average metric drop on common VLM tasks; 2–3× KV/memory savings; tangible energy gains from low-bit ops and step-shared scales.
- Deployability: Single-pass, per-input planning yields predictable latency for edge/real-time use (assistive tech, robotics, AR). Device-aware policies generalize across accelerators.
- Reliability: Safety backoff avoids catastrophic drops; alignment loss keeps retrieval/grounding on modality-general evidence.
- Transferability: Controller trained on one VLM family transfers to others with light calibration; extends to video/audio by gating spatiotemporal tokens, per-stream depth, and KV precision.
- Broader significance: Establishes sharedness and conditional entropy as actionable runtime signals and introduces QVI as a practical tool for training discrete, budget-aware inference planners—complementary to static pruning/quantization and depth-only exiting.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: Q-SHIFT — Device-aware, budget-conditioned multi-axis inference planner for VLMs

Overview
We propose three complementary experiments that validate Q-SHIFT along accuracy–latency tradeoff, internal signal utility (sharedness and conditional entropy), and decoder-time adaptivity (KV policies), including device-aware generalization and safety backoff. All are feasible in Python with PyTorch.

Common setup
- Base model: LLaVA-1.5-7B or BLIP-2 Vicuna-7B (HF transformers). Vision encoder frozen. Train only: SharednessHead, PrecisionAdapters, KVAdapters, HaltingGates, tiny fusion MLP controller, and risk head (<1% params). Optionally calibrate activation ranges on 5k samples.
- Data: Mixture (instruction-tuning + tasks). For quick reproduction:
  - VQA v2 (50k val subset), NLVR2 (dev), COCO captions (val2017), Flickr30k retrieval (test), 2k mixed-instruction prompts for long generations.
- Hardware: A100 80GB (dev), T4 16GB (generalization test). CUDA, PyTorch 2.3+, transformers, bitsandbytes, accelerate. Optional: TensorRT/TVM for oracle microbenchmarks.
- Latency measurement: torch.cuda.Event timing with warmup=10, iters=30; synchronize. Energy (optional): nvidia-smi --query-gpu=power.draw --loop-ms.
- Quant options: W8A8, W6A6, W4A4 via fake quantization for training; int kernels optional for eval; bitsandbytes int8 fallback.
- Reproducibility: seed=42, deterministic flags, gradient checkpointing off for timing.

Experiment 1 — End-to-end budget-conditioned multi-axis planning
Goal: Demonstrate Q-SHIFT yields better accuracy vs wall-clock latency than single-axis or static baselines under device-aware budgets.

Baselines
- Full-precision, full-depth (Upper bound on accuracy).
- Static PTQ: Q-VLM-like W8A8 fixed, full depth.
- Depth-only early exit: MuE-style halting trained with supervision; full precision.
- Static token routing: ToMe/SPARC-like merge at fixed ratio.
- Naive combo: grid of fixed token keep ratio + fixed depth + W8A8 + full KV.

Q-SHIFT variants
- Ours (full): joint token routing, depth/halting, mixed precision, KV schedule; planner trained with RQVI→QVI; latency oracle in loop; safety backoff on.
- Ablations: without sharedness (random routing), without entropy (fixed precision/depth), without KV schedule, without safety backoff, without oracle (FLOP proxy).

Training (controller + adapters only)
- Warm pass: 1–2 shallow layers per modality compute s(i) and conditional entropy features.
- Planner: latent z→ logits over discrete codebooks:
  - Token keep ratio {25,50,75,100%}, ToMe granularity {2,4,8}
  - Depth per modality {1/4,1/2,3/4,full}
  - Precision per block {W8A8,W6A6,W4A4}
  - KV precision {8,6,4} bits; head-drop on low mass; eviction policy on long sequences
- Latency oracle: predicted latency of action tuple used in loss; hard check at runtime.
- Objective: L = L_task + λb·(Latency−B)_+ + λm·MemReg + λe·ExitReg + λkv·KVReg + L_align. Budget B sampled curriculum: {p50,p60,p70,p80} of baseline latency.
- QVI: start 2 epochs with RQVI (bias-reduced) using M=8 tuples; switch to QVI with K=6 quantization points per lever; mixture-weighted loss with exact backprop; no REINFORCE.

Evaluation protocol
- For each task: sweep target budgets B∈{90%,80%,70%,60%,50% of baseline latency}. For each B:
  - Report: Accuracy (task-specific), latency (ms), speedup, ETRR, memory (KV MB), budget adherence rate (|lat−B|/B<5%), backoff frequency, energy (optional).
  - Plot accuracy vs latency and AUC (higher is better).
- Also report throughput (images/s) on batch=1 and batch=4 to show packing overheads.

Expected outcomes
- Q-SHIFT attains 35–60% latency reduction with ≤1–2% metric drop relative to FP baseline; superior to all single-axis baselines at same latency; high adherence rate (>95%) via oracle; lower KV footprint (2–3×) and fewer catastrophic failures due to safety.

Python sketch (condensed)

# Pseudocode; aligns with PyTorch/transformers
class SharednessHead(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, img_feats_l1, txt_feats_l1, attn_maps):
        # cross-attn agreement (JS), SPARC grouping, co-saliency
        s_txt = compute_sharedness_txt(attn_maps)
        s_img = compute_sharedness_img(attn_maps)
        ent_stats = compute_conditional_entropy(img_feats_l1, txt_feats_l1)
        return s_txt, s_img, ent_stats

class Planner(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, codebooks, oracle): ...
    def forward(self, features, budget_B):
        logits = self.fusion_mlp(features)
        # Quantized Variational Inference: select small set of tuples T and weights w
        tuples, weights = qvi_select(self.codebooks, logits)
        pred_latency = sum(w * self.oracle.predict(t) for w,t in zip(weights,tuples))
        return tuples, weights, pred_latency

def qshift_forward(batch, budget_B, train=True):
    s_txt, s_img, ent = sharedness_head(...)
    feats = build_features(s_txt, s_img, ent, lengths, budget_B)
    tuples, w, pred_lat = planner(feats, budget_B)
    # mixture execute: run each tuple deterministically on disjoint token packs
    outputs = mixture_execute(model, batch, tuples, w, safety_backoff=True)
    L_task = task_loss(outputs)
    L = L_task + lb*(pred_lat - B).clamp_min(0) + regs(...)
    return L

# Training loop
for step, batch in loader:
    B = sample_budget()
    loss = qshift_forward(batch, B, train=True)
    loss.backward(); opt.step()

# Eval loop with hard plan (argmax under latency <= B)
plan = planner.greedy_plan(feats, B, oracle, safety=True)
outputs = execute_plan(model, batch, plan)

Building the latency oracle
- Microbenchmark kernels affected by: seq_len_text, seq_len_img, token_keep_ratio, depth, precision, KV bits, head-drop, ToMe granularity, batch size.
- Create a design matrix X of action tuples and measure wall-clock y on target device. Fit a small regressor (XGBoost or linear + pairwise interactions) per batch size.
- Export to TorchScript for fast inference in training. Verify R^2>0.95 on held-out tuples.


Experiment 2 — Probing and ablation of sharedness and conditional entropy signals
Goal: Show MFH-derived signals predict which tokens/patches matter and when low-bit/early-exit is safe, and that they are necessary for planner gains.

Part A: Signal fidelity
- Compute s(i) and conditional entropy H(A_{l+1}|A_l) on held-out mixed data.
- Ground-truth token/patch importance:
  1) Leave-one-out occlusion for patches and word-drop for tokens: Δ loss/accuracy.
  2) Gradient×Input saliency or attention rollout as a proxy.
- Metrics:
  - Correlation: Spearman/Kendall between s(i) and importance; ROC-AUC for top-K important tokens.
  - Predictability: Correlate low H(A_{l+1}|A_l) with (a) small activation drift ||A_{l+1}−f(A_l)||, (b) low degradation when using W4A4 in that block, (c) safe early exit measured by intermediate CE.

Part B: Planner ablations
- Replace s and entropy with: (i) random noise; (ii) uniform; (iii) text-only attention; (iv) shuffled across samples.
- Retrain planner for 1–2 epochs under same budgets; evaluate as in Exp1 at B=70%,60%.
- Expectation: Significant drop in AUC and adherence without signals; token-selection precision@K decreases.

Python sketch for signals

# Sharedness via JS divergence of cross-attn
def sharedness_js(attn_img2txt, attn_txt2img):
    p = attn_img2txt.mean(dim=head) # [T_text, P_img]
    q = attn_txt2img.transpose(-1,-2).mean(dim=head) # [T_text, P_img]
    m = 0.5*(p+q); js = 0.5*(kl(p,m)+kl(q,m))
    s_txt = 1 - normalize(js, per_token=True)
    return s_txt

# Conditional entropy via fake quantization
def cond_entropy(A_l, A_lp1, n_bins=16):
    ql = fake_quantize(A_l, n_bins)
    qlp1 = fake_quantize(A_lp1, n_bins)
    p = joint_histogram(ql, qlp1)
    H = H_conditional(p) # H(qlp1|ql)
    return H

# Token importance via leave-one-out
def token_importance(model, batch, idx):
    out_full = model(batch)
    out_drop = model(drop_token(batch, idx))
    return loss(out_drop)-loss(out_full)

Reporting
- Table: correlation stats; ROC-AUC for top-K tokens and patches.
- Plot: degradation vs bitwidth conditioned on entropy bins; early-exit error vs depth when entropy is low vs high.


Experiment 3 — Decoder-time adaptivity: KV schedule + device generalization + safety backoff
Goals:
- Show long-sequence gains from KV precision/head-drop/eviction with minimal quality loss.
- Verify device-aware generalization using the oracle (train on A100 profile; test on T4 profile without retraining weights).
- Validate safety backoff prevents catastrophic failures.

Datasets
- Long captioning: COCO Captions with generation length 64–128 tokens, and an instruction set for detailed image descriptions and step-by-step reasoning prompts (~5k prompts).

Procedures
A) KV schedule effectiveness
- Compare: Full KV FP16; Static KV int8; Q-SHIFT KV schedule (bits {8,6,4}, head-drop when attention mass on high-s tokens, eviction for low-attn tokens after step t0).
- Metrics per generated token: latency/token, cumulative latency, peak KV memory, quality (CIDEr/BLEU/ROUGE or perplexity surrogate), attention-shift robustness (measure drops when attention moves to new regions).
- Expect: 1.8–3× KV memory reduction, 20–40% decoding latency cuts with <1 point CIDEr drop.

B) Device-aware generalization
- Train controller with A100 oracle; freeze.
- Build T4 oracle via microbench; at test time, planner uses T4 oracle without weight changes.
- Evaluate budget adherence and accuracy vs latency on T4 across B∈{90,80,70,60%}.
- Expect: adherence >90% and small AUC loss vs retraining; FLOP-based proxy fails to meet budgets.

C) Safety backoff study
- Construct adversarial/OOD subset: corrupted images (Gauss noise, occlusion 20–40%), ambiguous queries, counterfactuals from NLVR2.
- Run with and without safety backoff. Record catastrophic error rate (e.g., >10% logit margin drop or wrong binary), backoff trigger rate, and latency overhead of backoff.
- Expect: 30–60% reduction in catastrophic errors with <5% latency overhead.

Python sketch for KV adapters and safety

class KVAdapter:
    def __init__(self, bit_options=(8,6,4)):
        self.quantizers = {b: KVQuantizer(bits=b) for b in bit_options}
    def step(self, K, V, plan, attn_stats):
        bits = plan.bits_for_step
        head_mask = plan.head_mask(attn_stats)
        Kq, Vq = self.quantizers[bits](K[head_mask], V[head_mask])
        evict_idx = plan.evict_indices(attn_stats)
        Kq = evict(Kq, evict_idx); Vq = evict(Vq, evict_idx)
        return Kq, Vq

class SafetyBackoff:
    def __call__(self, state):
        if low_margin(state) or entropy_drift(state):
            state.raise_precision(); state.unmask_tokens(); state.defer_exit()
        return state

# Per-token latency measurement during decoding
for t in range(T):
    torch.cuda.synchronize(); start.record()
    logits, state = step_decode(...)
    end.record(); torch.cuda.synchronize()
    lat_ms = start.elapsed_time(end); log(lat_ms, kv_mem())

Reporting
- Plots: latency/token vs step; KV memory vs step; adherence rate across devices.
- Tables: Quality vs speed for KV baselines and Q-SHIFT.

Practical tips
- Start with W8A8 and only a subset of codebooks to stabilize; add W6A6/W4A4 after range calibration on 5k samples.
- Token packing: implement with index_select and block-sparse masks; measure Pytorch overhead; optionally pre-compile TensorRT engines for W8A8.
- Oracle: fit one model per batch size and precision regime; periodically re-calibrate if kernels change.
- Mixed precision kernels: if no fused kernels available, simulate speed via oracle penalty in training and evaluate wall-clock with PyTorch kernels plus a correction factor learned from microbenchmarks.

Success criteria (summary)
- Exp1: Dominant accuracy–latency AUC; 35–60% latency cuts for ≤2% drop; adherence >95%.
- Exp2: Strong correlation between s/entropy signals and true importance/predictability; ablations show clear degradation.
- Exp3: 2–3× KV memory reduction with small quality loss; adherence transfers across devices; safety reduces catastrophic errors with minimal overhead.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
Q-SHIFT — Device-aware, budget-conditioned multi-axis inference planner for VLMs

This specification instantiates the New Methods with a concrete, end-to-end experimental plan in PyTorch. It responds to each item in Experiment Strategy, consolidating overlapping checks where possible, and includes code sketches to accelerate reproduction.

Common setup

- Models
  - Prefer LLaVA-1.5-7B (llava-hf/llava-1.5-7b-hf) or BLIP-2 Vicuna-7B. Vision encoders remain frozen. Train only: SharednessHead, PrecisionAdapters, KVAdapters, HaltingGates, tiny fusion MLP planner, and a risk head (<1% trainable params total).
  - Token merging: Tome (https://github.com/facebookresearch/ToMe) via the tome-pytorch package when available; fallback to timm’s token pruning/merging utilities.

- Datasets
  - VQA v2 val (50k subset), NLVR2 dev, COCO captions val2017, Flickr30k test, plus ~2k mixed instruction prompts including long generations (64–128 tokens).
  - Preprocessing: follow llava-hf/BLIP-2 processors; max text length 384 for input, 128 for generation; pad/truncate accordingly.

- Hardware and software
  - Dev: A100 80GB, CUDA 12.x, PyTorch 2.3+, transformers, datasets, bitsandbytes, accelerate, xgboost, scikit-learn, timm, tome-pytorch, torchmetrics, numpy, pandas. Generalization: T4 16GB.
  - Seeds and determinism: seed=42; torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True); cudnn.benchmark=False; cudnn.deterministic=True. Turn off gradient checkpointing during timing.

- Quantization and precision
  - Training-time: simulate W8A8/W6A6/W4A4 via torch.ao.quantization.fake_quantize modules with per-tensor or per-channel affine observers (EMA). Optionally FP8 for A via torch.cuda if supported.
  - Inference-time: use bitsandbytes LLM.int8 for W8; simulate lower bits with fake-quant + oracle for timing; optionally plug TensorRT-LLM/TVM kernels if available.
  - Range calibration: 5k samples across all tasks before enabling W6/W4. Cache observer state.

- Latency measurement and energy
  - Use torch.cuda.Event with warmup=10, iters=30 per configuration. Synchronize before and after timing. Optional energy via nvidia-smi --query-gpu=power.draw with 100ms loop for coarse estimates.

- Logging
  - Use wandb or tensorboard; log latency, memory, accuracy, backoff events, and planner choices. Persist exact plans per input.

Core components (used across experiments)

1) SharednessHead and entropy features

- Computes per-token sharedness s(i) and per-layer conditional activation entropy H(A_{l+1}|A_l) from the first 1–2 transformer blocks of each modality.
- Signals
  - Cross-attention agreement: Jensen–Shannon divergence between head-averaged image→text and text→image attention, symmetrized and inverted to produce sharedness (higher = more modality-general).
  - Sparse grouping: SPARC-style minimal grouping approximated using Tome merges for vision tokens and per-token cosine similarity between grouped vision vectors and text tokens.
  - Co-saliency tie-breaker: head-averaged attention concentration overlap on top-k regions.
  - Conditional entropy: fake-quantize A_l and A_{l+1} (n_bins≈16) and compute H(ql+1|ql) from joint histograms.

Code sketch (hooks + features)

```python
import torch, torch.nn as nn, torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.ao.quantization.fake_quantize import FakeQuantize
from torch.ao.quantization.observer import MovingAverageMinMaxObserver

class SharednessHead(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_bins=16, topk=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.fq_l = FakeQuantize(observer=MovingAverageMinMaxObserver, dtype=torch.quint8,
                                 qscheme=torch.per_tensor_affine, reduce_range=True)
        self.fq_lp1 = FakeQuantize(observer=MovingAverageMinMaxObserver, dtype=torch.quint8,
                                   qscheme=torch.per_tensor_affine, reduce_range=True)
        self.n_bins = n_bins
        self.topk = topk

    @torch.no_grad()
    def sharedness_js(self, attn_img2txt, attn_txt2img):
        # Expect [B, heads, T_text, P_img] and [B, heads, P_img, T_text]
        p = attn_img2txt.mean(dim=1)  # [B, T, P]
        q = attn_txt2img.transpose(-1, -2).mean(dim=1)  # [B, T, P]
        m = 0.5 * (p + q) + 1e-6
        kl_pm = (p * (p.add(1e-6).log() - m.log())).sum(dim=-1)
        kl_qm = (q * (q.add(1e-6).log() - m.log())).sum(dim=-1)
        js = 0.5 * (kl_pm + kl_qm)  # [B, T]
        s_txt = (1.0 - (js - js.min(dim=-1, keepdim=True).values) /
                 (js.max(dim=-1, keepdim=True).values - js.min(dim=-1, keepdim=True).values + 1e-6))
        return s_txt  # higher is more shared

    @torch.no_grad()
    def cond_entropy(self, A_l, A_lp1):
        ql = self.fq_l(A_l)
        qlp1 = self.fq_lp1(A_lp1)
        # Histogram over flattened activations
        B = A_l.shape[0]
        ql = (ql.detach().flatten(1))
        qlp1 = (qlp1.detach().flatten(1))
        # Uniform binning over observed range
        def hist2d(x, y, bins=16):
            x = (x - x.min(dim=1, keepdim=True).values) / (x.max(dim=1, keepdim=True).values - x.min(dim=1, keepdim=True).values + 1e-6)
            y = (y - y.min(dim=1, keepdim=True).values) / (y.max(dim=1, keepdim=True).values - y.min(dim=1, keepdim=True).values + 1e-6)
            xb = torch.clamp((x * (bins - 1)).long(), 0, bins - 1)
            yb = torch.clamp((y * (bins - 1)).long(), 0, bins - 1)
            H = torch.zeros(B, bins, bins, device=x.device)
            idx = xb * bins + yb
            H.scatter_add_(2, idx, torch.ones_like(idx, dtype=torch.float))
            H = H / (H.sum(dim=(1, 2), keepdim=True) + 1e-6)
            return H
        H = hist2d(ql, qlp1, bins=self.n_bins)
        P_x = H.sum(dim=2, keepdim=True)  # [B, n_bins, 1]
        P_y_given_x = H / (P_x + 1e-6)
        H_cond = -(H * (P_y_given_x.add(1e-6).log())).sum(dim=(1, 2))  # [B]
        return H_cond

    def forward(self, attn_img2txt, attn_txt2img, A_l_img, A_lp1_img, A_l_txt, A_lp1_txt):
        s_txt = self.sharedness_js(attn_img2txt, attn_txt2img)
        s_img = s_txt.mean(dim=1, keepdim=True).expand_as(A_l_img[..., 0])  # proxy if needed
        H_img = self.cond_entropy(A_l_img, A_lp1_img)
        H_txt = self.cond_entropy(A_l_txt, A_lp1_txt)
        return s_txt, s_img, {
            'H_img': H_img, 'H_txt': H_txt,
            'H_stats': torch.stack([H_img, H_txt], dim=-1)
        }
```

Note: in practice, attach forward hooks to early blocks to capture cross-attention weights and activations, then feed to SharednessHead once per input.

2) Planner (QVI) and latency oracle

- Inputs: budget B, histograms/percentiles of s and H, sequence lengths, optional device tag.
- Codebooks (hardware-aligned discrete levers)
  - Token routing: keep ratios {0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0}; Tome merge size {2, 4, 8} for low-s tokens.
  - Depth/halting: per modality {1/4, 1/2, 3/4, 1.0}.
  - Mixed precision: per-block W/A ∈ {W8A8, W6A6, W4A4}.
  - KV policy: bits ∈ {8, 6, 4}; head-drop threshold; eviction onset step t0.
- QVI training
  - Parameterize each lever with a low-D latent z -> logits over discrete choices.
  - RQVI (epochs 0–1): choose M action tuples via low-discrepancy grids over softmax logits; compute mixture-weighted objective to reduce variance.
  - QVI (epochs 2+): use fixed quantization points per lever; enumerate K tuples closest to current logits; compute mixture loss L = Σ_k w_k L(task, tuple_k) + λ terms; backprop through logits and fusion MLP.
- Latency oracle
  - Fit an XGBoost regressor on microbenchmarked tuples to predict latency given: text_len, img_len, keep_ratio, merge_size, depth_text, depth_vision, precision_id, kv_bits, head_drop_frac, eviction_step, batch_size.
  - During training/eval, compute predicted latency and add constraint penalty λb*(lat − B)_+; also enforce a hard cap at runtime with safety backoff.

Code sketch (planner + oracle)

```python
import xgboost as xgb
from itertools import product

class LatencyOracle:
    def __init__(self, booster):
        self.booster = booster
    @torch.no_grad()
    def predict(self, features_dict):
        import numpy as np
        cols = [
            'text_len','img_len','keep_ratio','merge_size','depth_text','depth_vision',
            'precision_id','kv_bits','head_drop','evict_step','batch_size']
        X = np.array([[features_dict[c] for c in cols]], dtype=np.float32)
        return float(self.booster.predict(xgb.DMatrix(X))[0])

class Planner(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, oracle, d_in=64):
        super().__init__()
        self.oracle = oracle
        self.fusion = nn.Sequential(nn.LayerNorm(d_in), nn.Linear(d_in, 64), nn.GELU(), nn.Linear(64, 32), nn.GELU())
        # logits per lever
        self.token_keep = nn.Linear(32, 4)     # {0.25,0.5,0.75,1.0}
        self.merge_sz = nn.Linear(32, 3)       # {2,4,8}
        self.depth_t = nn.Linear(32, 4)
        self.depth_v = nn.Linear(32, 4)
        self.precision = nn.Linear(32, 3)      # {8,6,4}
        self.kv_bits = nn.Linear(32, 3)
        self.head_drop = nn.Linear(32, 3)      # {0.0,0.25,0.5}
        self.evict = nn.Linear(32, 4)          # {0,16,32,64}

    def qvi_select(self, logits_dict, K=6):
        # Build candidate tuples by taking top-2 per lever and Cartesian product; pick K best by score proxy
        import itertools
        idxs = {k: torch.topk(v, k=min(2, v.shape[-1]), dim=-1).indices.tolist() for k,v in logits_dict.items()}
        cand = list(itertools.product(*idxs.values()))
        # Score proxy: sum logits
        scores = []
        keys = list(logits_dict.keys())
        for tup in cand:
            s = sum(logits_dict[k][i] for k,i in zip(keys, tup))
            scores.append(s.item())
        order = torch.tensor(scores).argsort(descending=True).tolist()[:K]
        tuples = [tuple([idxs[k][t[j]] for j,k in enumerate(keys)]) for t in [list(range(len(keys)))]*K]
        # uniform weights as a simple quadrature; can refine by softmax of scores
        weights = torch.ones(len(order), device=logits_dict[keys[0]].device) / len(order)
        return tuples, weights

    def forward(self, feat_vec, budget_B, meta):
        h = self.fusion(feat_vec)
        logits = {
            'keep': self.token_keep(h).squeeze(0),
            'merge': self.merge_sz(h).squeeze(0),
            'dt': self.depth_t(h).squeeze(0),
            'dv': self.depth_v(h).squeeze(0),
            'prec': self.precision(h).squeeze(0),
            'kv': self.kv_bits(h).squeeze(0),
            'hd': self.head_drop(h).squeeze(0),
            'ev': self.evict(h).squeeze(0)
        }
        tuples, weights = self.qvi_select(logits, K=6)
        # Predict latency for each tuple
        pred_lat = 0.0
        plans = []
        keep_vals = [0.25,0.5,0.75,1.0]
        merge_vals = [2,4,8]
        depth_vals = [0.25,0.5,0.75,1.0]
        prec_vals = [8,6,4]
        kv_vals = [8,6,4]
        hd_vals = [0.0,0.25,0.5]
        ev_vals = [0,16,32,64]
        for t_idx in range(len(weights)):
            k_idx, m_idx, dt_idx, dv_idx, p_idx, kv_idx, hd_idx, ev_idx = tuples[t_idx]
            features = {
                'text_len': meta['text_len'], 'img_len': meta['img_len'],
                'keep_ratio': keep_vals[k_idx], 'merge_size': merge_vals[m_idx],
                'depth_text': depth_vals[dt_idx], 'depth_vision': depth_vals[dv_idx],
                'precision_id': prec_vals[p_idx], 'kv_bits': kv_vals[kv_idx],
                'head_drop': hd_vals[hd_idx], 'evict_step': ev_vals[ev_idx],
                'batch_size': meta['batch_size']
            }
            L = self.oracle.predict(features)
            pred_lat += weights[t_idx] * L
            plans.append((features, weights[t_idx].item()))
        return plans, pred_lat

    @torch.no_grad()
    def greedy_plan(self, feat_vec, budget_B, meta):
        plans, _ = self.forward(feat_vec, budget_B, meta)
        # Choose best plan under budget; if none, pick min latency
        under = [p for p in plans if p[0]['pred'] if 'pred' in p[0] else 0]
        best = None
        best_lat = float('inf')
        for (f, w) in plans:
            lat = self.oracle.predict(f)
            if lat <= budget_B and lat < best_lat:
                best_lat, best = lat, f
        if best is None:
            # fallback to min latency
            best = min(plans, key=lambda x: self.oracle.predict(x[0]))[0]
        return best
```

3) PrecisionAdapters, KVAdapters, HaltingGates, Safety backoff

- PrecisionAdapters wrap attention/linear ops with pre-instantiated fake quant modules during training; route token packs to appropriate bitwidth paths.
- KVAdapters provide int8/6/4 storage with per-head scales and optional head-drop; eviction removes lowest-attention tokens after a planned step.
- HaltingGates drop remaining blocks per modality at the planned depth; decoder schedules precision upgrades as needed.
- Safety backoff monitors logit margins and entropy drift; if triggered, it raises precision, unmasks tokens, or defers exits.

Code sketch (packed execution + safety)

```python
class PrecisionAdapters:
    def __init__(self, bit_options=(8,6,4)):
        self.bit_options = bit_options
        self.act_fq = {b: FakeQuantize(observer=MovingAverageMinMaxObserver,
                                       dtype=torch.quint8, qscheme=torch.per_tensor_affine)
                       for b in bit_options}
    def quantize(self, x, b):
        if b == 8:  # can use bitsandbytes ops for weights
            return x
        return self.act_fq[b](x)

def pack_by_mask(x, mask):
    # x: [T, D], mask: [T] bool; returns packed_x, idx, rev_idx for unpack
    idx = mask.nonzero(as_tuple=False).squeeze(-1)
    rev = torch.full((x.size(0),), -1, device=x.device, dtype=torch.long)
    rev[idx] = torch.arange(idx.numel(), device=x.device)
    return x.index_select(0, idx), idx, rev

class KVAdapter:
    def __init__(self, bits=(8,6,4)):
        self.bits = bits
    def quantize(self, K, V, b):
        if b == 8:
            return K, V
        # simple per-tensor affine quant (simulate)
        def q(x):
            scale = x.abs().amax(dim=(-1,-2), keepdim=True) / (2**(b-1)-1 + 1e-6)
            qx = torch.clamp((x/scale).round(), -(2**(b-1)), 2**(b-1)-1)
            return qx*scale
        return q(K), q(V)

class SafetyBackoff:
    def __init__(self, margin_thr=0.5, drift_thr=0.2):
        self.margin_thr = margin_thr
        self.drift_thr = drift_thr
    @torch.no_grad()
    def maybe_backoff(self, state):
        if state['logit_margin'] < self.margin_thr or state['entropy_drift'] > self.drift_thr:
            state['precision'] = max(8, state['precision'])
            state['keep_ratio'] = min(1.0, state['keep_ratio'] + 0.25)
            state['depth_text'] = 1.0; state['depth_vision'] = max(0.75, state['depth_vision'])
            state['kv_bits'] = max(8, state['kv_bits'])
            state['backoff'] = True
        return state
```

Latency oracle microbenchmarks (shared across experiments)

- Build a design matrix X of action tuples; measure wall-clock latency y.
- Fit XGBoost regressor per batch size; ensure R^2 > 0.95 on held-out tuples.

Code sketch (bench + fit)

```python
import random, time, numpy as np, xgboost as xgb

def microbench(model, tuples, repeats=30):
    # tuples: list of dicts with all features
    times = []
    for t in tuples:
        # Prepare synthetic inputs with specified lengths and precision; run forward-only
        x = torch.randint(0, 32000, (t['text_len'],), device='cuda').unsqueeze(0)
        img = torch.randn(1, 3, 336, 336, device='cuda')
        # Warmup
        for _ in range(10):
            _ = model(img, x)
        start, end = torch.cuda.Event(True), torch.cuda.Event(True)
        torch.cuda.synchronize(); start.record()
        for _ in range(repeats):
            _ = model(img, x)
        end.record(); torch.cuda.synchronize()
        lat = start.elapsed_time(end)/repeats
        times.append(lat)
    return times

def fit_oracle(df):
    dtrain = xgb.DMatrix(df[cols].values, label=df['latency'].values)
    params = {'max_depth': 6, 'eta': 0.1, 'subsample': 0.8, 'colsample_bytree':0.8,
              'objective': 'reg:squarederror', 'tree_method':'hist'}
    bst = xgb.train(params, dtrain, num_boost_round=500)
    return bst
```

Training loop outline (common)

```python
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(filter(lambda p: p.requires_grad, model.parameters()), lr=2e-4, weight_decay=0.01)

for epoch in range(E):
    for batch in loader:
        budget_B = sample_budget()  # from {p50,p60,p70,p80} baseline
        # 1) Warm pass to compute features
        s_txt, s_img, ent = sharedness_head(attn_i2t, attn_t2i, A_l_img, A_lp1_img, A_l_txt, A_lp1_txt)
        feat_vec = build_features(s_txt, s_img, ent, budget_B)
        meta = {'text_len': batch['text_len'], 'img_len': batch['img_len'], 'batch_size': batch['bs']}
        # 2) Planner (QVI/RQVI)
        plans, pred_lat = planner(feat_vec, budget_B, meta)
        # 3) Execute mixture on disjoint token packs (approximate by weighted loss)
        total_loss = 0.0
        for (plan, w) in plans:
            outputs = execute_plan(model, batch, plan)  # applies routing, depth, precision, KV policy
            L_task = task_loss(outputs, batch)
            total_loss = total_loss + w * L_task
        L = total_loss + lambda_b * torch.clamp(pred_lat - budget_B, min=0) + regs()
        opt.zero_grad(); L.backward(); opt.step()
```

Experiment 1 — End-to-end budget-conditioned multi-axis planning

Goal
- Validate that Q-SHIFT outperforms single-axis and static baselines on accuracy–latency tradeoffs under device-aware budgets, and adheres to budgets with high reliability.

Baselines
- Full FP, full depth (upper bound accuracy).
- Static PTQ: fixed W8A8 (Q-VLM-like), full depth, no routing.
- Depth-only early exit: MuE-style halting; full precision, no routing, no KV schedule.
- Static token routing: Tome/SPARC at fixed keep ratio 0.5.
- Naive combo: grid search over {keep 0.5, depth 0.75, W8A8, KV8} without planning.

Q-SHIFT variants
- Full method: routing + depth + mixed precision + KV schedule; latency oracle in loss; RQVI→QVI; safety backoff on.
- Ablations: minus sharedness, minus entropy, minus KV policy, minus safety, minus oracle (FLOP proxy).

Training protocol
- Optimizer: AdamW lr=2e-4, weight_decay=0.01; 3–5 epochs over mixed data; mixed precision autocast for speed.
- Curriculum budgets B sampled per batch from empirical latency percentiles {p80,p70,p60,p50} of FP baseline.
- Regularizers
  - MemReg: L2 on KV footprint vs target; KVReg: penalty for excessive head-drop or bits<6 when H high.
  - ExitReg: monotone precision schedule and staged halting; penalize changes across steps.
  - L_align: contrastive loss to protect high-s tokens (ITC/ITM style), weight 0.05–0.1.
- RQVI for first 1–2 epochs (M=8 tuples), QVI thereafter (K=6 tuples). Use oracle for predicted latency in loss; enforce hard runtime checks.

Evaluation protocol
- For each task {VQA, NLVR2, COCO cap, Flickr retrieval} and budgets B ∈ {90%, 80%, 70%, 60%, 50% of FP baseline latency}:
  - Report: task metric (Acc/CIDEr/Recall@K), mean latency (ms), speedup, Expected Time Reduction Rate (ETRR), KV memory (MB), budget adherence rate (|lat−B|/B < 5%), backoff frequency, optional energy.
  - Throughput at batch=1 and batch=4.
  - Aggregate accuracy–latency AUC per task and averaged.

Reliability enhancements
- 30 timing repeats; 95% CI via bootstrap over batches.
- Oracle calibration check every epoch on 64 random tuples; if R^2 < 0.9, refit or increase features.
- Backoff audit: log triggers and net latency impact per input; ensure <5% overhead.

Expected outcomes
- 35–60% latency reduction with ≤1–2% metric drop; adherence >95%; KV footprint 2–3× smaller; fewer catastrophic errors vs baselines.

Illustrative execution function (routing + depth + precision + KV)

```python
def execute_plan(model, batch, plan):
    # Apply token routing using sharedness: keep top-k text tokens; Tome for vision
    keep_ratio = plan['keep_ratio']; merge_size = int(plan['merge_size'])
    s_txt = batch['s_txt']  # from SharednessHead
    T = s_txt.shape[1]
    k = max(1, int(T * keep_ratio))
    top_idx = torch.topk(s_txt.squeeze(0), k=k, dim=-1).indices
    # Build masks and pack
    mask = torch.zeros(T, dtype=torch.bool, device=s_txt.device)
    mask[top_idx] = True
    # Precision schedule
    pbits = int(plan['precision_id'])
    kv_bits = int(plan['kv_bits'])
    # Depth (per modality)
    depth_txt = plan['depth_text']; depth_vis = plan['depth_vision']
    # KV head-drop / eviction
    head_drop = plan['head_drop']; evict_step = int(plan['evict_step'])
    # Forward with hooks that honor plan
    outputs = model.forward_with_plan(batch, mask, merge_size, pbits, kv_bits,
                                      depth_txt, depth_vis, head_drop, evict_step)
    return outputs
```

Experiment 2 — Probing and ablation of sharedness and conditional entropy signals

Goals
- Validate that MFH-derived signals correlate with token/patch importance and low-entropy regions are safe for low-bit and early-exit decisions.
- Show these signals are necessary for planner gains.

Part A: Signal fidelity
- Procedure
  1) Compute s(i) and H(A_{l+1}|A_l) on a held-out split (2–5k samples across tasks).
  2) Ground truth importance via:
     - Leave-one-out (LOO): drop one textual token or occlude one image patch; measure Δloss or Δmetric.
     - Gradient×Input or attention rollout as a proxy (cheaper secondary signal).
  3) Metrics
     - Spearman/Kendall correlation between s(i) and LOO importance per sample and averaged.
     - ROC-AUC for detecting top-K important tokens/patches.
     - Predictability: bin blocks by entropy (quartiles) and measure: activation drift ||A_{l+1}−f(A_l)||; degradation under W4A4 vs W8A8; early-exit intermediate CE.

- Analysis integration into plan: map low-entropy regions to aggressive precision (W4/W6) and early-exit; verify error curves flatten in low-entropy bins.

Code sketch (LOO importance + correlation)

```python
from torchmetrics.functional import auroc

@torch.no_grad()
def token_importance(model, batch, token_idx):
    out_full = model(**batch)
    loss_full = task_loss(out_full, batch)
    batch_drop = drop_token(batch, token_idx)
    out_drop = model(**batch_drop)
    loss_drop = task_loss(out_drop, batch)
    return (loss_drop - loss_full).item()

@torch.no_grad()
def evaluate_sharedness_correlation(model, dataset, sharedness_head):
    spearmans = []
    rocs = []
    for batch in dataset:
        s_txt, s_img, ent = run_sharedness(model, batch, sharedness_head)
        T = s_txt.shape[-1]
        imp = torch.tensor([token_importance(model, batch, i) for i in range(T)])
        rho = torch.corrcoef(torch.stack([s_txt.flatten().float(), imp.float()]))[0,1].item()
        # Binary labels for top-10% most important
        k = max(1, int(0.1*T))
        labels = torch.zeros(T, dtype=torch.long)
        labels[torch.topk(imp, k).indices] = 1
        roc = auroc(s_txt.float(), labels.float(), task='binary').item()
        spearmans.append(rho); rocs.append(roc)
    return np.mean(spearmans), np.mean(rocs)
```

Part B: Planner ablations
- Replace signals with: random noise, uniform, text-only attention, or shuffled across samples.
- Retrain planner for 1–2 epochs under B ∈ {70%, 60%}; keep all else identical.
- Evaluate as in Experiment 1 (AUC, adherence, token-selection precision@K).

Expected outcomes
- High correlation (Spearman > 0.4–0.6) and ROC-AUC > 0.7 for top-K importance with s(i).
- Low entropy bins correlate with safe W4A4 and early exits (≤0.5% additional error vs W8A8 within bin), strong separation vs high-entropy bins.
- Ablations degrade AUC and adherence significantly (≥5–10% AUC drop), with poor token selection.

Experiment 3 — Decoder-time adaptivity: KV schedule, device generalization, safety backoff

Goals
- Demonstrate long-sequence gains from planned KV precision/head-drop/eviction.
- Show device-aware generalization using only a new oracle (no retraining).
- Validate safety backoff reduces catastrophic failures with small overhead.

Datasets
- Long captioning/QA: COCO captions val2017, and 5k detailed/step-wise prompts; generate 64–128 tokens.

Procedures
A) KV schedule effectiveness
- Compare:
  - FP16 KV (upper bound quality, worst memory/time).
  - Static KV int8 (bitsandbytes or per-tensor int8 sim), no head-drop.
  - Q-SHIFT planned KV: bits {8,6,4} across steps; head-drop when attention mass concentrates on high-s tokens; eviction of low-attn tokens after planned t0.
- Metrics (per token and aggregate): latency/token curve, cumulative latency, peak KV memory, CIDEr/BLEU/ROUGE (captioning) or perplexity surrogate, attention-shift robustness (quality drop when attention distribution shifts > threshold mid-generation).
- Expectation: 1.8–3× KV memory reduction and 20–40% decoding latency cuts for ≤1 CIDEr drop.

B) Device-aware generalization
- Train planner on A100 with A100 oracle; freeze controller.
- Build T4 oracle via microbench; at test, use T4 oracle with the same weights.
- Evaluate adherence and accuracy–latency on T4 for B ∈ {90,80,70,60%}.
- Expectation: adherence >90% without retraining; FLOP proxies fail to meet budgets consistently.

C) Safety backoff study
- Construct OOD/adversarial set: Gaussian noise, partial occlusions (20–40%), ambiguous queries, NLVR2 counterfactuals.
